_meta.description: Hand-curated reference set for the roasting domain: canonical roast profiles spanning the classic light-to-dark taxonomy, plus notable specialty roasters. Temperatures are bean-probe degrees Celsius, times are seconds, and development_time_ratio is development time (first crack to drop) divided by total roast time. Each profile carries a suitable_for rule that the loader resolves against var_varieties to derive RoastProfile → suitableFor → Variety edges: species lists the bean species the profile suits, and min_optimal_altitude requires the variety's optimal_altitude_max to reach the threshold (high-grown beans are denser and retain the acidity that lighter profiles showcase).
_meta.source: Public roasting literature: Sweet Maria's roast level guide, SCA roast terminology, and conventions popularized by Scott Rao's The Coffee Roaster's Companion. Values are representative midpoints, not any roaster's proprietary curve.

profiles.name: Nordic Light
profiles.description: Scandinavian-style filter roast dropped shortly after first crack. Maximizes origin character, florals, and bright acidity; best with dense, high-grown washed lots that can withstand a short development without tasting grassy.

profiles.name: Cinnamon Roast
profiles.description: The lightest conventional roast, ended at the very start of first crack. Pale cinnamon-brown surface, pronounced sourness and tea-like body; mostly used for cupping evaluation and showcasing exceptional high-altitude lots.

profiles.name: City Roast
profiles.description: Roast taken through first crack and dropped soon after it ends. Balanced sweetness with origin acidity still in front; the everyday specialty filter roast for quality Arabica.

profiles.name: City+ Roast
profiles.description: A touch more development past first crack, rounding acidity into caramel sweetness while keeping origin character legible. A forgiving middle ground that suits most Arabica regardless of growing altitude.

profiles.name: Omni Roast
profiles.description: A modern specialty compromise developed enough to extract as espresso but light enough to shine as filter. Slightly longer development than a pure filter roast to keep espresso shots from tasting sour.

profiles.name: Full City Roast
profiles.description: Developed to the verge of second crack with light oil sheen possible. Bittersweet chocolate and caramel dominate; origin acidity recedes. The classic American medium-dark drip roast.

profiles.name: Full City+ Roast
profiles.description: Into the first snaps of second crack. Roast character and body take over from origin character, which makes it tolerant of softer lots and Robusta components in blends.

profiles.name: Classic Espresso
profiles.description: Traditional espresso development: slow Maillard phase and a long post-crack stretch for solubility, syrupy body, and crema stability. Works for straight Arabica and the Arabica/Robusta blends of southern-European tradition.

profiles.name: Vienna Roast
profiles.description: Through the first phase of second crack with visible surface oil. Dark chocolate, light smoke, and heavy body; bean origin matters less than density, so it accepts both species.

profiles.name: French Roast
profiles.description: Well into second crack: shiny, dark-brown beans with pronounced carbonized bittersweetness. Roast flavor entirely supersedes origin, making it a leveling roast for blends of any composition.

profiles.name: Italian Roast
profiles.description: The darkest conventional roast, taken near the end of second crack. Smoky, ashy bitterness with thin acidity and maximum body; the historical base of southern-Italian espresso blends, frequently Robusta-heavy.
